title: Explorations in Mongolia: a review of the Central Asiatic expeditions of the American Museum of Natural History
review:
Narrative style account of a 1925 expedition into Mongolia. From Kalgan, via Pekin, they travelled (innovatively using cars) into the north-west region. This expedition was motivated by Henry Osborn's theory that the high plateau of Central Asia was a cradle for many mammalian species. Around 40 people were involved in the interdisciplinary work; moving through the Gobi Desert with 125 camels and 7 cars. Four units focused on specific tasks. 

 New topographic maps were produced as previous ones were based on unreliable proverbial accounts. They used speedometers as a way to measure distance, and topographers, it seems, were mapping as they were travelling, using cars as reference points for their stadia rods. At the beginning palaeontology was heavily emphasized. The expedition established the region was the home of most dinosaurian reptiles from the Jurassic to Pleistocene periods. This is evidently an outdated assertion to make nowadays. They obtained over a thousand fossil specimens. Andrews highlights a few, and mentions that the dinosaur eggs discovered in this expedition sparked popular interest at the time. 

 Archaeology is also reviewed. Andrews explains that traces of the presence of prehistoric cultures were found across the expedition's entire route. For example: burial structures, necklaces ornamented with dinosaur egg shells, and so on. The Gobi Desert was inhabited by humans during the Stone Age. For botany, over 500 living plants were collected, and palaeobotanical evidence hinted at the existence of a sequoia forest, although parts of the region were desertic even then. Geologically, results on a giant batholith, climate change, and stratigraphic columns are mentioned. They also stole tens of thousands of animals. 

 The pictures are quite nice, it really looks like there was a lot of flat terrain (which RCA says made car travel possible), with cliffs and other rocky mountainous surfaces. Quite an enjoyable read, I like how it seems almost mundane compared to how explorers are portrayed in media, although RCA does say that the expedition was described as luxurious.

title: Monitoring in Small Firms: Experimental Evidence from Kenyan Public Transit
review:
I hated this. This is some Big Brother shit, I am not crazy about RCTs to begin with but this is incredibly dystopic imo. L RCT. All the focus is on the firm outcomes, why should I care that rentier prinicpals can't grow this specific type of business? I am of the idea that the mechanism they describe and the environment it necessitates is a very specific sector of the economy. Transfers between principal and agents are welfare neutral, and the increase of the principal contract value while the agent's contract value decreases is framed as efficiency and not distribution. I cannot see how the intervention's gains, if scaled, would drive any sort of growth or justify the former relocation I describe. Monitoring constraints are a problem for firm expansion, but these firms are all in the public transportation sector, where market concentration is particularly undesirable, unless provided for free by the state. 

 The model is of course mathematically consistent. Two risk neutral individuals, principal and agent, interact repeatedly through a daily relational contract. The driver chooses effort $e$ and risky effort $r$, where effort is assumed to increase revenue with no cost in repairs, and risk damages the vehicle. Here already disaster: everybody knows increasing effort can absolutely conduce to damages that could imply repair costs. Agents suffer twice continuously differentiable disutility $\psi(e,r)$. Stochastic revenue is drawn $\tilde{y} \sim G(\cdot \mid e, r)$, where this distribution is bounded from below by minimum living income $w$, and $G$ is assumed to be twice continuously differentiable with respect to all arguments with density $g(y \mid e, r)$. Increasing risk or effort increases expected revenue. Revenue available for transfer to the nefarious principal is $y = \tilde{y} - w \in [0, \bar{y}]$. Here $w$ is exogenous and already we have embeded ideology as lack of ideology, but everybody knows this false neutrality is capitalist ontology. Risk induces repair cost $c \sim F(\cdot \mid r)$. This implies $$\frac{\partial \mathbb{E}[c]}{\partial e} = 0$$ Because of this assumption repair cost condition FOC differ only in the risk dimension. Would you get into the matatu of a driver that has been driving for 24 hours? I doubt it. They impose an assumption that the owner's marginal willingness to trade effort for risk is always higher than the driver's over the relevant region. Defining the following incentive compatible set: $$\mathcal{I} = \left\{ (e, r) \in \mathcal{S} : \frac{\int_{0}^{\bar{y}} y\, g_e(y \mid e, r)\, dy}{\int_{0}^{\bar{y}} y\, g_r(y \mid e, r)\, dy} = \frac{\psi_e(e,r)}{\psi_r(e,r)} \right\}$$ are the optimal bundles of effort and risk that agents chooses for whatever disutility they have. They then define the bliss point to be $$(e^D, r^D) = \arg\max_{(e,r) \in \mathcal{S}} \mathbb{E}[y \mid e, r] - \psi(e,r)$$ at which the assumption satisfies the following $$\frac{\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial e}}{\frac{\partial \Pi}{\partial r}} > \frac{\frac{\partial (\mathbb{E}[y]-\psi)}{\partial e}}{\frac{\partial (\mathbb{E}[y]-\psi)}{\partial r}}$$ and thus by construction excesssive risk (compared to a planner's social optimum) is guaranteed, that is $r^D > r^*$. Then, the owner owner maximizes expected value $V$ $$V = \max_{e,r,t(y),\,p(t)} \mathbb{E}\!\left[ t(y) - c + \delta V - [1 - p(t(y))] \, h \mid e,r \right]$$ subject to some acceptable constraints. This is the sum of expected transfers and continuation value $\delta V$ minus expected cost of firing and risk. Transfer meaning Principal does not observe $y$, and recieves transfer $t(y)$, while agent keeps income $y-t(y)$. After observing $t$, principal can choose to re-hire with probability $p(t)$. Replacing the agent costs $h$. Driver has an outside option $u$. The optimal transfer rule is then $t(y) = \min\{y, T\}$. Agent transfers up to $T$, the amount that guarantees re-employment. The optimal rehiring rule is $$p(t) = 1 - \frac{T - t}{\delta U - u}$$ where $\delta U$ is agent continuation value, which is destroyed if they lose their job. In the baseline case principal cannot punish risky behavior because they cannot observe risk, only transfers. With the introduction of Palantir style technology the principal can recondition the rehiring rule based on ton only effort but also risk. Thus the optimal rehiring rule given under monitoring becomes $$p(t,e,r)=\begin{cases} 1-\dfrac{T-t}{\delta \,\overline{u}_M - u}, & \text{if } e=e_M \text{ and } r=r_M \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ with owner mandated $(e_M, r_M)$. This reduces risk. The model predicts higher efforts, profits, and lower repair costs. We will discuss welfare implications shortly. 

 The following regression is run on daily panel data $$y_{id} = \alpha_d + \tau_{r(i)} + \sum_{m}^{6} D_{im}\beta_{m} + \mathbb{X}_i' \gamma + \varepsilon_{id}$$ I will copy and paste the main results: By month 6, treatment drivers spend 1.45 (9.8 percent) more hours on the road per day. This is a significant increase in a context where drivers already work ­ 14-hour days. Treatment drivers are approximately 400 meters closer to the licensed route than control drivers throughout the study. We also see the number of kilometers increase by 13 kilometers per day on average. Taking the average gains over the study period and extrapolating to the full year (assuming matatus operate 25 days a month), owners can expect a $1,200 increase in annual firm profits. We find weak statistical evidence that treatment firms are more likely to grow their business than control firms. The effect of the tracking device on driver ­ take-home pay is ambiguous. The impact depends on how revenue changes and how much the owner adjusts the target. End of copy paste. They then estimate the model with GMM, and find that They estimate that the driver's contract value decreases by $19.7 (SE $13.8). Meanwhile, owner contract value increases by $82 (SE $133), implying a total welfare increase of $63 (SE $137). These calculations are uniformative. 

 The normative implications rely on the fact that whatever welfare increases from robbing workers then some consumer benefit from risk reduction has to apply but this is a particularity of public transport and if this RCT would be applied to a different sector the monitoring welfare effects are incredibly worrying for the working class. In fact, the fact that the effort increases so much could actually be concerning, and the authors do mention possible safety externalities. They say in the appendix that a motivation was to see if this technology could improve safety. If workers end up overworking the effect is not clear. The authors are incredibly defensive about welfare dimensionality for drivers, as if relationship with the principal was comparable with your wage, give me a break. The survey that they run and its results imply self selection, and possible coercion is of course ignored. This is hyperreal experimentation with the subjugated, and once more capital exerts its tentacles through the intelligentsia to justify exploitation. The defense is that in this particular case monitoring reduced risky behavior, but insofar as the powers that be are concerned, this is green light to do things NSA style. 

 The model codes the driver's preference for risky driving as a wedge to be corrected, as if the bliss point were pathology rather rational adaptation to subsistence. The social planner welfare benchmark is only normatively appealing if the actual relevant externality is repair costs. If a companion study found that there was no reduction in accidents, it is hard to see how this is anything other than a formal restatement of the owner's objective function dressed in utilitarian clothing.

title: The Illiquidity of Water Markets
review:
This paper is incredible. It examines markets vs. quotas and finds that when there exists liquidity constraints quotas are more efficient. A natural question to ask is for generalizations of this idea, insofar as the structure of the mode of production itself is such that liquidity constraints are prevalent and exist almost by construction (competition, placing beings in relation, etc.) for household-firms. In this case, poor apricot farmers did worse that wealthy ones. Efficiency is computed in a clever fashion, as output data is not available. I write up their model in the following paragraphs. Farmers are heterogeneous in their willingness and ability to pay for water. The quotas are efficients because of the marginal returns to water. They state that in the presence of frictions, appropriate institutions need to be designed when it comes to allocating resources. With poor farmers and non-enforcable contracts, endogeneous borrowing constraints emerge. 

 The setting is the historic water auction in the city of Mula. It used to be, following the Reconquista of Murcial in the 1200s. Shareholders owned a cartel corporation in which an English (sequential outcry ascending price) auction sold water usage rights and dividends were paid at the end of the year. In 1966 the W syndicate helped establish quotas proportional to the size of the farmers' plots. Wealthy and poor farmers purchased similarly throughout the year, but there is a critical season for the trees where price increased dramatically. Poor farmers could not match what rich farmers were buying, and thus had to reduce their usage, which reduced output. 

 The model is as follows. There are $N$ rational and forward looking farmers $i$. There is moisture $M$, money $m$, and time $t$. Seasons ${\omega}_{t} \in \{1,2,\cdots, 53\}$ matter since demand is seasonal. The model runs forever with discount between periods denoted by $\beta \in (0,1)$, so today mattters more than the future. Water $j$ only brings utility during the critical season. Its supply is exogeneous to the farmer, and farmers can store it, although there is a probability of evaporation. Water is contained in the production function $h(j_{it},M_{it},\omega_{it};\gamma)$ of apricots that is increasing in moisture level $M_{it}$. Utility is profits and preferences are given by $$u_{it}(\omega_{it}, M_{it}, w_t, p_t, \varepsilon_{it}; \gamma, \zeta) = h(\omega_{it}, M_{it}, w_t; \gamma) - \zeta \mathbf{1}\{\omega_{it} > 0\} - p_t j_{it} + \varepsilon_{it}$$  with $j_{it} \in \{0,1,\dots,J\}$ being water bought by farmer $i$, $p_t$ is the price of water, and $\varepsilon_{it}$ is a random shock for farmer $i$, all at time $t$. $\mathbf{1}(\cdot)$ is an indicator function. $\zeta$ is a parameter for irigation costs, and  $\gamma$ is a parameter for the transformation rate of water into apricots during the fruit’s growth season. Every week the farmer decides how much water to buy to maximize apricot revenue minus water fixed and variable costs. Rainfall $r_t$ and the distribution $\sigma_{\varepsilon}$ both implicitly affect the profit equation. It is assumed that $$\forall j_{it} > 0 \Rightarrow (m_{it} - p_t j_{it}) \geqslant 0$$ which means that farmers cannot buy water if they do not have the liquid money for it: there is a missing credit market. Farmers are heterogeneous in $m_{it}$ and $\varepsilon_{it}$, both private information. Thus, farmers who are wealthy can always buy water, but poor farmers might have to buy water to store before price surges in the critical season, when they will be unable to do so. This dynamic system has the following state variables: how wet the soil is, what week of the year it is, the price of water/rainfall, cash holdings, and productivity shocks. 

 Suppose both wealthy and poor farmers do not differ in measurement error. Denote $\text{PW}$ as the point in which apricot trees die from low moisture ($\forall t \Rightarrow M_{it} \geqslant \text{PW}$), and $\text{FC}$ as full capacity to hold water. Moisture evolves weekly as follows:  $$M_{it} = \min \Big\{ M_{i,t-1} + r_{t-1} + \frac{j_{i,t-1} \cdot 432{,}000}{\text{area}_i} - \text{ET}(M_{i,t-1}, \omega_{t-1}), \text{FC} \Big\}$$ with 432,000 the number of liters contained in each $j$, are measured in square meters, and $\text{ET}(M_{i,t-1}, \omega_{t-1})$ the evapotranspiration function. As previously mentuoned, water has diminishing returns for a pair of reasons. One, the wetter the soil, the faster the water evaporates. Two, we can reach $\text{FC}$. Next, they describe farming as a deterministic clock that peaks at the critical season. They also link rainfall with the price of water. Droughts increase the price of water. The computer model draws $(p_t, r_{t-1})$ from previous years in the current iteration of simulacra. Empirical bootstrapping. Productivity shocks are drawn for the computer simulacrum i.i.d. from a Gumbel distribution with CDF $F(\varepsilon_{it}; \sigma_\varepsilon) = e^{-e^{-(\varepsilon_{it} / \sigma_\varepsilon)}}$. The variance is $\frac{\sigma_{\varepsilon}^2 \pi^2}{6}$. They then use Nested Logit to model the fact that irrigating 0 units of $j$ and irrigating $>1$ units of $j$ are the real choices, and not that buyig 0,1,2,3, and so on units of water with equal distinction. The nested tree separates between a branch that decides to buy water or not, and a branch that if buying, decides how many units to buy. The asusmption is made that $\varepsilon$ affects the buying water choices $(1,2,\cdots,J)$ together. The parameter to control how correlated the group choices are is $\lambda \in (0,1]$. Finally, the cash holdings cash balance is as follows: $$m_{it} = m_{i,t-1} - p_{t-1} j_{i,t-1} + \phi_{i0} + R_{it} + \nu_{it}$$ with $m_{i,t-1}$ being the starting balance, $p_{t-1} j_{i,t-1}$ the cost of water, $\phi_{i0}$ a baseline endowment, $R_{it}$ apricot revenue, and $\nu_{it}$ some random shock, an unexpected inheritance or burden and so on. Revenue $R_{it}$ is only collected on week 24, so $$R_{it} = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } \omega_t \neq 24 \\ \text{Rev}_{it} & \text{if } \omega_t = 24 \end{cases}$$ this means farmers need to make revenue last for a whole year. Revenue is given by the production that happened during the critical season (weeks 18-32): $$\text{Rev}_{it} = \sum_{\omega_t=18}^{32} h(j_{it}, M_{it}, \omega_t; \gamma)$$ We can write the production function as  $$h(j_{it}, M_{it}, \omega_t; \gamma) = \big[ \gamma \cdot (M_{it} - \text{PW}) \cdot \text{KS}(M_{it}) \cdot \text{Z}(\omega_t) \big]$$ with  $\text{KS}(M_{it}$ being a hydric stress coefficient, and $ \text{Z}(\omega_t)$ dummies for the critical season. Thus we can write the revenue function as $$\text{Rev}_{it} = \sum_{\omega_t=18}^{32} \gamma \cdot (M_{it} - \text{PW}) \cdot \text{KS}(M_{it})$$ The value function is given by $$\begin{aligned} V(&M_{it}, \omega_t, p_t, r_t, m_{it}, \varepsilon_{it}) \equiv \max_{j_{it} \in \{0,1,\dots,J\}} \Big\{ h(j_{it}, M_{it}, \omega_t; \gamma) - \zeta \mathbf{1}\{j_{it} > 0\} - p_t j_{it} + \varepsilon_{it} \\ &+ \beta \, \mathbb{E} \big[ V(M_{i,t+1}, \omega_{t+1}, p_{t+1}, r_{t+1}, m_{i,t+1}, \varepsilon_{i,t+1}) \mid M_{it}, \omega_t, p_t, r_t, m_{it}, \varepsilon_{it}, j_{it} \big] \Big\}, \\ &\text{s.t. } M_{it} \geqslant \text{PW}, \quad j_{it} p_t \leq m_{it}, \quad \forall j_{it} > 0 \end{aligned}$$ which is the monstrous Bellman equation. They calculate the parameters $\Theta \equiv (\gamma, \sigma_{\epsilon}, \zeta, \lambda)$ using two steps. First, they compute transition probability matrices for the following observable state variables: moisture, week, price, and rain., and then use transition matrices to forward simulate the value function. The simulacrum behaves like historical data. The authors exclude poor farmers to avoid estimating that they are choosing not to buy because the do not value the water, which would be a biased estimated demand elasticity. 

 They then do a welfare analysis. There are two ways of wasting water, due to heterogeneity (having water when they are unproductive), and due to decreasing marginal returns (DMR), recieving water when soil moisture is high. Then, they compare Quotas (allocate water units uniformly) with markets. This solves the DMR problem, no one has a super wet plot while the neighbor has a dry one, but it does not solve the heterogeneity problem. In a perfect world, markets are flawless, solve both problems: farmers only buy when soil is dry and they are javing a productive week. With liquidity constraints, markets fail and create both types of waste. From the text: quotas are more efficient than markets when there is large heterogeneity in wealth and small heterogeneity in productivity. Markets are more efficient in the opposite case. In the general case where there is heterogeneity in both wealth and productivity, the efficiency of markets relative to quotas is ambiguous. 

 They compare four different scenarios, a free market, naive quotas, smart quotas that randomizes water among the bottom X% of farmers (the post 1966 system), and a centrally planned scenario that they call highest valuation. The smart quota is better than the market and the naive quota, but worse than the high valuation. The authors also respond to possible counter-argumentation: running tests on if rich farmers are better than the poor, if poor farmers are risk averse, and so on. In reality, for winter crops there is no difference, high correlation between wealth and productivity does not change the ranking of allocations, and the data shows that even if the poor farmers are risk averse, that alone cannot explain the observed behavior, it HAS TO BE that there are liquidity constraints. They compare this scenario to Coase's ideas on vertical integration, and the quota system is perfectly rational. 

 The authors say: Markets might not be efficient when liquidity constraints are present. This is true. They also remind us that this change was not done for egalitarian purposes, it was a purely efficiency based measure. Thus, the usual planning (equality but on average people might be worse off) vs. markets (efficient but unequal) is not necessarily true, insofar as even within consequentialist ontology that through normative commitments derives markets as the best there is, there are still scenarios in which they do not achive this purpose, and even inside of this methodology we can encounter contradictions and counterargumentation. This paper was incredible. I am happy I read it.

title: A one-shot proof of Arrow’s impossibility theorem
review:
This is a short and concise proof of Arrow's theorem. It does this by identifying a pivotal voter who can alter social preference and then show they are a dictator. The statement of the theorem is the usual, it cannot be that there exists a SWF that satisfies Unanimity, AIIA, and Non-Dictatorship. 

 The proof is as follows. Suppose that $R$ is a SWF that satisfies $A_1$ and $A_2$. Let $\vec{\succsim}$ be fixed but arbitrary for which $\forall \: n \in \mathbb{N} \Rightarrow a_i \succsim_n a_j$. This means every single voter prefers $a_i$ to $a_j$. Because of property $A_1$ this means $a_1 \triangleright a_j$. Then imagine a *flipping* process where all voters switch from $a_i \succsim_n a_j$ to $a_j \succsim_n a_i$ so again by $A_1$ we have that $a_j \triangleright a_i$. Because of this flip it *has to be* that the machine switches $\triangleright$ after somebody's switch. We call this person $n_{ij}$ the ($i, j$)*-pivotal voter*. Because of $A_2$ no other $a_k$ alternative matters for this switch. 

 $$ \begin{array}{c|cccccc} & 1 & \cdots & n_{ij}-1 & n_{ij} & n_{ij}+1 & \cdots & N \\ \hline & a_j & \cdots & a_j & a_i & a_i & \cdots & a_i \\ \vec{\succsim}^{\prime} & a_k & \cdots & a_k &  &  & & \\ & a_i & \cdots & a_i & a_j & a_j & \cdots & a_j \\  & & & & a_k & a_k & \cdots & a_k \\ \hline & \square & \cdots & \square & &  & &  \\ & \square a_j & \cdots & \square a_j & a_j & a_i & \cdots & a_i \\ \vec{\succsim}^{\prime \prime} & \square & \cdots & \square &  & \square  & \cdots & \square \\ & a_i & \cdots & a_i & a_i & \square a_j & \cdots & \square a_j \\ &  &  & & a_k & \square   & \cdots & \square \end{array} $$ Now look at the scenario above. We first have $\vec{\succsim}^{\prime}$ and three alternatives $a_i, a_j, a_k$. We are at the point before the *pivotal voter's* flip. This person's rankings are currently $a_i \vec{\succsim}^{\prime}_{n_{ij}} a_j \vec{\succsim}^{\prime}_{n_{ij}} a_k$. Because they are the pivotal voter we have that 50.1% of voters prefer $a_i$ to $a_j \Rightarrow a_i \triangleright a_j$.  If you look at the table you will notice every single persona agrees $a_j \succsim a_k$ so the voting machine outputs $a_j \triangleright a_k$. Thus we have $a_i \triangleright a_j \triangleright a_k$, which matches the *pivotal voter's* preference. Now imagine $\vec{\succsim}^{\prime \prime}$ and we can represent possible $a_k$ rankings by $\square$. If you look at the table, every single voter that is not $n_{ij}$ can rank $a_k$ wherever they please in a way that does not change how they rank $a_i$ and $a_k$. The *pivotal voter* ha snow flipped and now ranks  $a_j \vec{\succsim}^{\prime \prime \prime}_{n_{ij}} a_i \vec{\succsim}^{\prime \prime}_{n_{ij}} a_k$. Since they are the point of the flip the machine now also flips to $a_j \trianglerighteq a_i$. Remember, nobody has changed how they compare $a_i$ and $a_k$. Because of property $A_2$ this means that we still carry $a_i \trianglerighteq a_k$ from ${\succsim}^{\prime} \Rightarrow$ $$\forall \: i \neq j \neq k \Rightarrow a_j \succsim_{n_{ij}} a_k \Longrightarrow a_j \trianglerighteq a_k$$ If we look at the squares we don´t know anything on how people rank $a_j$ and $a_k$. It could be that everybody has $a_k \succsim a_j$. But we have $a_j \triangleright a_k$ because of ONE person. This violates $A_3$. 

 The paper is very good, althought maybe it could use with clearer language at times.

title: War Porn
review:
This essay is about the images revealed by CBS in 2004 depicting torture and prisoner abuse in Abu Ghraib. The Hooded Man being the most famous. Here B begins by comparing this with 9/11, declaring the first a banality, insofar as it represents pornographic staged humilliation, a parody of violence, and the latter a major event that revealed vulnerability in the hitherto invincible hegemon. B says war turns itself into a grotesque (...) reality-show, in a desperate simulacrum of power. 

 Left without enemies, drunk with power, Miss Columbia has no other option but to humiliate herself. This is very much of actuality at the time of this review, and truly at any other time. Here we have a theory of power that explains the current state of self-destruction that reigns the imperial cores. 

 B argues that the images themselves are secondary to the spectacle they produce, as they have become as virtual as war itself. This has rendered image to go from representation to pornography that exposes the absolute degradation present in the systems that proclaim themselves a beacon of freedom and democracy and so on. Because this other does not fear death,  the objective is for it to be exterminated symbolically. B says the goal of the war is not to kill or to win, but abolish the enemy. 

 Add 22 years and replace the q in Iraq with an n for Iran. Or switch 9/11 to October 7, and the images to ones of the Gaza Genocide. Current events reproduce this symbolic violence.

title: The Pyres of Autumn
review:
This feels very relevant. It is both immigrants and natives who are disaffiliated and dettached from cultural belonging. Both turn their disaffiliation into defiance and go onto the offensive. The native offense is of course late 2010s and 2020s style groyperism. Or whatever adjacent fascistic disorder. The immigrant offense is precisely the setting of the essay, the 2006 youth protests in France. 

 B claims the superiority of Western culture is sustained only by the desire of the rest of the world to join it, and that it is a the fantasy that raising the rest of the world to Western living standards will settle matters. It is impossible to do so, the mechanisms at play only reinforce Westernism. How can you integrate when you are in the process of disintegration? 

 B rejects the notion that these riots are a symptom of failed integration, or any other economic malaise: it is symbolic, the destruction of the commodity, which is the representation of modularity in consumer society is then, repudiation of the social order. How can the West stop terrorism, mass immigration, and all these issues that trouble the minds of MAGA voters, when the threats are endogenous to the system rather that exterior imposition? This is an internal contradiction that characterizes hegemony.

title: Manufacturing revolutions: Industrial policy and industrialization in South Korea
review:
There is a famous Ariel Rubinstein motto that goes: *I have not seen any paper in Economics which deserves more than 15 pages*. Unfortunately most papers are around 30 pages, and this one in particular is about 50 pages with tables and figures, this is just too much. Excessive robustness sections, repetition of arguments, and so on. This paper compares changes in outcomes between targeted (treated) and non-targeted (control) manufacturing industries. Targeted HCI (heavily-chemical industries) expanded output by 100%, labor productivity was 15% higher, both in comparison to non-targeted manufacturing sectors. There is evidence for dynamic learning by doing and longer term dynamic comaprative advantage of targeted export industries. 

 There is presence of bourgeois ideology on page 1688, where it is said that the Nixon Doctrine *unfortunately* coincided on growing military antagonism from the DPRK. It is argued that the 1973 ROK industrial drive was security driven and catalized by both of these crises. The president at the time was Park Chung Hee, a corporatist anti-communist conservative. According to the New Yorker, Park´s background included a *period of flirtation with a group of South Korean Communists, whom he betrayed*. We can make a counter-point to the paper´s sepculative ideation and posit an ideological motivation for Park´s industrial policy, which is that there was a genuine paranoia surrounding the Juche idea, and a *dirigist* desire to see ROK grow. Industrialization is thus, in this view, an ideological project, and we can describe both parties as military antagonic. Historically this episode is preceded by the Korean DMZ Conflict (Second Korean War) in 1966-1969.

 The HCI drive targeted steel, nonferrous metals, shipbuilding, machinery, electronics, and petrochemicals. The state targeted upstream heavy industries instead of final weapons production. There was some early skepticism from inventors, and it is argued that this is why targeting was not endogeneous of sectors that would have grown regardless. This drive in reality consisted of a bundle of policies: investmet and trade. The investment part was essentialy subsidized credit, around half of domestic credit was policy loans. For trade it mainly consistes of exceptions aimed at HCI, such as import duties being waived. After Park was assasinated in 1979, the incentive structure was dismantled and liberalization was pursued. Lane makes use of a short panel (1970-1986) and a log panel (1967-1986), with a trade-off between industry observations and time periods. 

 There is a discussion of two externalities that justify industrial policy under neoclassical ideology. The first is dynamic economies of scale, where production generates learning-by-doing which raises future productivity. When learning spills over across firms within an industry, individual firms do not internalize the full social return to production, leading to underinvestment. Even without learning-by-doing spillovers, firms can still underinvest when faced with capital constraints that prevent them from financing early losses despite long-run returns. The second is linkage effects, basically upstream effects on inputs producers, and downstream forward linkages. The main specification is the following linear TWFE difference-in-differences (DD) regression: $$\ln(y_{it}) = \underbrace{\alpha_i + \tau_t}_{\text{Fixed and Time Effects}} + \sum_{j \neq 1972} \beta_j \big(\text{Targeted}_i \times \text{Year}_t^j\big) + \sum_{j \neq 1972} \mathbf{X}_i^{\top} \times \text{Year}_t^j \Omega_j + \varepsilon_{it}$$ with industry $i$ and year $t$. Figure II is the DD estimates for the positive effect on output. The paralell trends seem credible despite the short pre-trend. Figure III shows point estimates (yearly) on labor productivity (up), prices (down), employment (up), output and labor share (up), and number of plants (up). It also provides estimates for export devlopment variables, all positive. The results are checked for robustness using Sant'Anna and Zhao (2020) and Callaway and Sant'Anna (2021) double-robust estimators. The ATT estimates are fairly similar to TWFE ones, amd coefficient differences seem to come from distinction between short and long panels. The long panel has lower coefficient estimates for the variables of interest. We then switch to a triple-differences (DDD) setup, where ROK is compared with other countries, while keeping the targeted and non-targeted manufacturing industries as treatment and control. Here we need strong paralell trends: the differential evolution between HCI and non-HCI must have also been the same in the absence of treatment across countries. $$Y_{ict} = \underbrace{\alpha_{it}}_{\text{industry-year}} + \underbrace{\tau_{ct}}_{\text{country-year}} + \underbrace{\sigma_{ci}}_{\text{country-industry}} + \sum_{j \neq 1972} \beta_j \big(\text{Korea}_c \times \text{HCI}_i \times \text{Year}_t^j\big) + \varepsilon_{ict}$$ The results show higher point estimates during for the export development variables, but is even higher following the 1979 liberalization. Lane mentions that there have been *no shortage of failures* in industrial policty, while here we have arguably a success. I would take it a step further, it is not because an alternative has failed in $x$ amount of historical settings that disqualifies it from consideration. The author also estimates outcome variables for the policy mechanisms: 81.4% increase in total investment, and 109% increase in input outlays. An attempto to assess the presence of learning by-doing forces the following equation is estimated: $$Y_{it} = \beta_1 \text{Experience}_{it} + \beta_2 \text{Experience}_{it} \times \text{Targeted}_i + \theta \text{Size}_{it} + \alpha_i + \tau_t + \mathbf{X}_{it}^{\top} + \varepsilon_{it}$$ Experience is defined as cumulative production. The author is clear that this is not causal and is purely indicative and correlational. This is also true of all following estimations. $beta_2$ is of interest, as it identifies the differential effect for the treatment group. The effects show a negative relationship between experience and unit cost reduction, and a positive relationship between experience and TFP. The sign persists for the treatment and experience interaction, suggesting HCI sectors have steeper experience-TFP relationship. It would seem more accumulated output is associated with better performance. Again, this is not causal. Finally, the author uses IO tables to construct backward and forward linkages (how much industry supplies or is supplied by HCI sectors). The following regression is estimated: $$\ln(y_{it}) = \alpha_i + \tau_t + \sum_{j \neq 1972} \gamma_j \big(\text{Backward Linkage}_i \times \text{Year}_t^j\big) + \sum_{j} \delta_j \big(\text{Forward Linkage}_i \times \text{Year}_t^j\big) + \varepsilon_{it}$$ Here the treatment is continuous. The interactions $\gamma_j$ and $\delta_j$ capture the *differential evolution of industries with strong versus weak exposure* to HCI sectors. The results show that industries with strong forward linkages to HCI sectors had a more robust development. Essentially industries that were using HCI inputs grew more, which I can imagine was one of the mechanisms through which the military industrial complex was to be benefitted, as intended by Park. The effect of backward linkages was more limited. There is then a SUTVA section that reads like a response to a referee report. 

 That is the end of the paper's scope. There is no mention of social cost (Onsan illness) and so on, something that Choi and Levchenko (2025) attempt. Maybe I can read it in the future. For me the main weakness, and it´s not the author´s fault (he did as much as he could), is that there is no clear way of distinguishing what *worked*, insofar as there is no way of separating the effect of the elements of the policy bundle to the bundle itself, as it is the combination of all measures that is defined as the treatment. It is hard then to prescribe policy based on this, it could have been that one particular element was responsible for all the effects and we simply do not know. However, the paper is very strong, and it shows strong evidence for re-examining knowledge that is considered to be canonical or of *common sense*. I like that it demystifies the idea of economic *miracles*, and shows that interventionism can steer entire industries to incredible improvements.

title: Philosophy and the Black Panthers
review:
This is mostly about Huey P. Newton and his relationship with philosophy, in particular his theoretical contributions to the distinction between revolution and resistence. The revolutionary projeect thrives on the acceleation of time, while resistence involves a sustained and prolonged time span. It is the people who make revolution, and it is the oppressors who cause resistence. The objective of the enemy is to then dismantle the *capacity* to resist through installations of terror and spectacles of humilliation. The distinction can be traced to the schism between Kantian modal categories of possibility and actuality. One is the problem of realizing freedom and the other is the problem of forceful opposition.

 It is the job of the vanguard party to provide leadership, and its responsibility is to provide strategic conduct for resistence. It was through COINTELPRO that the FBI was able to transform the Black Panthers from an organization that provded communal meals, healthcare, education, to a *pantomime* revolutionary enemy. It was during Neewton's imprisonment that the insurrectionary rhetoric of Eldridge Cleaver flourished. This tactical stratagem was critiqued by Newton, as well as Cleaver's homophobic views. Following Cleaver's departure, the Black Panthers returned to working on building capacity to resist. Cleaver, funniliy enough, became a conservative Republican later in his life. 

 There is a lot on Newton's relationship to Plato, his reading of *Phaedo*, his history with *Republic* as an educational tool in his youth. The article ends with a discussion on Nietzche and how Newton regognized that the *will to power* was the basic drive of man. The Black Panthers used the *Genealogy of Morals* as a strategic manual that helped them use words as revaluing instruments or compromising weapons.  This is a good and rather short piece..

title: Bats from Mount Whitney, California
review:
This very short paper describes a few taxonomies of bats in Mount Whitney, California. The author is Glover Morrill Allen, who accompanied Thomas Lyman to a short expedition from Lone Pine to the upper limit of timber. There, they camped for a week at 11,000 feet (3350 meters) by the outlet of a lake. From Google Maps it seems Mount Whitney is quite rocky, although there seems to be some pines and other vegetation at the base. 

 I did not know this, but to obtain specimens zoologists would kill them and collect the cadaver. The main contribution is the discovery of a new species of bat, *myotis albicinctus*. This bat is described as smilar to *myotis lucifugus*, better known as a little brown bat, first described bt John Eatton Le Conte. Allen describes the new species as having whiter border on the wing memabranes, and two larger premolars in the upper jaw. The pelage of the animal is described as very clear and pale sandy above pale buff, which is a color in *Color standards and color nomenclature* by Robert Ridgway. Using a second specimen from A. Brazier Howell, since the skull of the one they caught was lost somehow, Allen confirms the difference in the premolars. He claims this is *unquestionably* a new species. 

 Three other bats are described, a Yuma myotis (*myotis yumanensis sociabilis*), a long-egged myotis (*myotis volans longicrus*), and a big brown bat (*Eptesicus fuscus*). The paper then tries to corroborate Hilda Wood Grinnell's idea that during summer adult males leave a female-dominated colony and go to higher elevations. Since all five specimens obtained at such heights were males, it would seem to support this theory. The explanation given is that there is a temporary increase in insect life, thus causing a temporary invasion of non-breeding bats. This is because upper levels of mountains abound with insects. There is also some discussion of life zones as developed by C. Hart Merriam. It would seem this new species did not survive this older style taxonomy, as the mammaldiversity website lists *myotis albicinctus* as a synonym of *myotis lucifugus*.

title: Libertarian Paternalism
review:
Another very short piece. I like to read two or three pages of other papers when I am reading a longer, more difficult one. 

 There is some useful propositions here that would be helpful to central planners who wish to remain non-coercive. In this respect, small examples are given of how to steer people gently and ways to modify behavior that do not seem to impact too much on the liberty of the participants of social organization. The argument is mostly amde to show that arguments against paternalism are not satisfactory. There is some discussion on how choices are not rational, and how this rationality is violated all the time when placed under scrutinity. 

 There is an example given of a cafeteria, where the paternalistic decision is to place the healthy food at the beginning and deserts at the end, since people´s choices are affected by the ordering. Then, another example is given here, in that maybe the deserts whould be placed in another table, to increase the transaction cost. This is a more intrusive intervention, but they argue it remains libertarian, since choice is not really constrained. I liked the bits about how there is bias for status-quo. However, the framework remains within the biggest status-quo of them all, market allocations and revelaed preferences, welfare, and so on. We cannot judge this paper too hard as it is only a discussion piece.

title: The New Economics of Industrial Policy
review:
There has been a revival of industrial policy in economics. Previously there were negativa and vicious reactions from mainstream economists against this idea. Industrial policy has many forms but can always be defined as creating incentives for private-sector behavior to act in a manner that is consistent with some notion of structural change. There are three rationales given for industrial policies. 

 The first are positive externalities of learning, national security, and of good-jobs. These are best handled by Pigouvian subsidies. The second is the coordination market failure, where individual producer's profitability may depend on other producers down or upstream the linkage chain. It may be that there are suboptimal equilibriums when there is no coordination. This is best resolved by governments calling on firms to be together or make simultaneous investments. Finally, we have public inputs, in which governments are *doomed to choose* (insofar as having to make choices of building infrastructure, prioritizing *x* or *y* training, etc.)what activities are more deserving of these goods or inputs. These require the provision of the government, evidently. 

 At the side of these strong cases for industrial policy, here are also strong critiques. They usually center around political capture and information shortcomings. The first describes lobbying, corruption, and so on affecting the interventions in a way that distorts social profit into the benefit of these self-interested actors, and the second says that governments are not expected to know enough of the previous market failures to correctly act upon them. There are other more theoretical critiques, for example, if inelastic demand leads to modest aggregate welfare gains, despite strong external economies of scale, it can be said that industrial policy does not lead to structural change. 

 It is difficult to measure industrial policy. The first is that it requires many tools, that usually change over time. Some scholars conflate industrial policy with protectionist trade policy, and this is not really the case in contemporary settings. There have been efforts to distinguish government expenditures, the use of ML to classify policy descriptions, etc. This allows researchers to capture policy that does not require spending, examples of coordination policy, distinguish industrial policy from other policy, etc. These efforts show that industrial policy is on the rise, with higher-income countries being major users. In lower-income countries it may be that fiscal constraints bind fast for industrial policy that requires spending. Countries tend to do the same across the income distribution. 

 They then start with he empirical challenge in evaluating industrial policy. Let $g(\theta)=(1-\theta)A$ be the government performance as a negative function of a parameter of market failure $\theta \in (0,1)$, with $A$ being some state variable affecting performance. Teh greater the market failure, the larger the gap between social and private returns. Let $s \in [0,1]$ be a subsidy that comes with some fiscal cost $\varphi \alpha (s)$ with $\alpha$ being a rising and convex function of $s$ and $\varphi$ being the ability to intervene effectively. The subsidy alleviates the gap to $(1-\theta)[1-s]$. Then, with industrial policy we have growth being $$g(s,\theta,\varphi)=(1-\theta(1-s))A-\varphi\alpha(s)$$ The optimal level of the subsidy $s^{\text{soc}}$ is the value of $s$ that solves $g_{s}(s,\theta,\varphi)=\theta A - \varphi^{\prime} \alpha(s) = 0$. However, there are other motivations for the subsidy. The government can choose to channel resources to favored groups, Salinas de Gortari style. Let $\pi(s)$ be a single-peaked, concave function, with max value $\pi(s^{\text{pol}})$, that represents political benefits to the government of using the subsidy. Assume government behavior maximizes a value function $$u(s;\theta,\varphi)=\lambda g(s,\theta,\varphi)+\pi(s)$$ where political and economic objectives enter, the latter with weight $\lambda$. The government chooses the level of subsidy $s^{\text{gov}}$ which is the solution to the FOC $$\lambda g_{s}(s,\theta,\varphi)+\pi^{\prime}(s) = \lambda [\theta A - \varphi \alpha^{\prime}(s)]\pi^{\prime}(s)=0$$ We can now consider how observational data can help us see if industrial policy works. It would ideally help us distinguis three views: governments can succesfully identify and support firms that will enhance whatever objective (developmentalist), governments try to support firms but do so inappropriately (inefficacy), and governments do not seek desirable outcomes and are basically corrupt (rent-seeking). We only observe how $g$ varies with $s$. The first generation of empirical work was mostly correlational, and infered industrial policy was mostly ineffective. However, with this model, the lower the $\lambda$, the larger the subsidy and the lower the rate of growth. This confirms rent-seeking. Howeverm if the only determinant of the policy was market falures $\theta$, then governemnt is a social-welfare maximizer. In this case, internention is higher with higher market failure, and the larger the market failure the lower the growth rates, as socially optimal policy does not totally offset the market failures. If you notice, this is the same pattern as the rent-seeking government, despite this particular government being the opposite. Thus, observational data can be misleading. 

 The modern empirical toolkit is better suited at answering and evaluating the efficacity of implementation. There is new evidence of this kind on sectoral industrial policy, maybe one of the most controversial types. There is a new crop of studies, in particular Lane's paper on HCI drive, and Juhász's article on the Napoleonic blockade, that seem to support the infant industry hypothesis. It could be that temporary protection changed the long-term profitability of production across regions. There is also some work that finds that tacit knowledge was an important component of technology transfer. They believe the literature must move towards questions of implementation, as studies are too abstract to inform policy in any way. Even Lane's QJE paper cannot distinguish from the HCI bundle what was it that led to increased comparative advantage in South Korea. There is urgent need of more work informing what makes for effective sectoral industrial policy. Public R&D may have a place in the toolkit, and that place-based industrial policy can shift the composition of local economic outcomes. There is also some work on Liu (2019) using a network model to show that the optimal sectors to target are those whose imperfections are the most consequential given their impact through input-output linkage. This is all ina section analysing the East Asian miracle. There is then some welcomed sociology from Peter Evans, who argues fro embedded autonomy, which is that close links between private and public sectors may not always lead to capture, but might be necessary in ensuring proper information to design policy. There is some more on Bartik and training being more effective than tax credits, and on the fact that there is no reason to be solely concerned with manufacturing when it comes to industrial policy. 

 Overall a great article, easy to read, and very relevant to the work I am currently doing.

title: The Impact of Trade on Intra-Industry Reallocations and Aggregate Industry Productivity
review: We start with a dynamic industry model with heterogeneous firms. There is a representative consumer with a CES utility function over continuous good $\omega \in \Omega$ $$U = \left[ \int_{\omega \in \Omega} q(\omega)^{\rho} \, d\omega \right]^{1/\rho}$$ Consumers have *love of variety* for every $\omega \in \Omega$. Goods are substitutes $\Rightarrow \rho \in (0, 1)$. Elasticity of substitution is $\sigma = 1/(1-\rho)$. If one good is too expensive then consumers will switch. We know from Dixit and Stiglitz (1977) that consmer behavior can be modeled considering the set of varieties consumed as $Q \equiv U$. The model creates an aggregate price, a weighted average of all prices $$P = \left[ \int_{\omega \in \Omega} p(\omega)^{1-\sigma} \, d\omega \right]^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}}$$ and we can derive optimal consumtion and expenditure decisions $$\begin{aligned}&& q(\omega) = Q \left[\frac{p(\omega)}{P}\right]^{-\sigma} && r(\omega) = R \left[\frac{p(\omega)}{P}\right]^{1-\sigma}\end{aligned}$$ where $R = P Q = \int_{\omega \in \Omega} r(\omega)\, d\omega$. There is a continuum of firms, all producing different varieties $\omega \in \Omega$. Production only requires labor, which is inelastically supplied. $L$ is its aggregate level which is an index of the size of the economy. Labour is $\ell = f + \tfrac{q}{\varphi}$ where $q$ is output, $f \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$ is a fixed cost, and productivity levels are indexed by $\varphi \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$. This is the formula for costs of production. $\tfrac{q}{\varphi}$ is the variable cost. With higher productivity $\varphi$, the firm is able to produce one unit of $q$ with less labor. We can also think of productivity as producing higher quality variety at equal cost. Firms choose markup $$\frac{\sigma}{\sigma - 1} = \frac{1}{\rho} \Rightarrow p(\varphi) = \frac{w}{\rho \varphi} = \frac{1}{\rho \varphi}$$ where $p(\varphi)$ is the pricing rule and $w=1$ is the wafe. Profit is then $$\pi(\varphi) = \underbrace{r(\varphi)}_{\text{revenue}} - l(\varphi) = \underbrace{\frac{r(\varphi)}{\sigma}}_{\text{variable profit}} - f$$ A higher $\varphi$ firm will have larger output $q$ and revenue $r$, lower price $p$, and higher profit $\pi$ than firms with lower productivity $\varphi$. Melitz characterizes equilibirum of $M$ firms and goods. The distribution is $\mu(\varphi)$ over productivity levels over a subset of $[0, \infty)$. The pricing rule is $$\begin{split}P = \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} p(\varphi)^{1-\sigma} M \mu(\varphi)\, d\varphi \right]^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} \\ = M^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} p(\varphi)^{1-\sigma} \mu(\varphi)\, d\varphi \right]^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} \\ = \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} p(\varphi)^{1-\sigma} \mu(\varphi)\, d\varphi \right]^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} \\ = p(\tilde{\varphi}) \Rightarrow P = M^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} p(\tilde{\varphi})\end{split}$$ where $\tilde{\varphi}$ is a weighted average of firm productivity $$\tilde{\varphi} = \left[ \int_{0}^{\infty} \varphi^{\sigma-1} \mu(\varphi)\, d\varphi \right]^{\frac{1}{\sigma-1}}$$ We need a weighted average because higher productivity firms need a heavier weight as they produce a larger share of total output. From this *average* firm we can derive total revenue, output, profit and overall price level of the entire industry $$\begin{aligned}P = \underbrace{M^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}} p(\tilde{\varphi})}_{\text{price}} && R = \underbrace{M \, r(\tilde{\varphi})}_{\text{revenue}} && Q = \underbrace{M^{\frac{1}{\rho}} q(\tilde{\varphi})}_{\text{output}} && \Pi = \underbrace{M \, \pi(\tilde{\varphi})}_{\text{profit}}\end{aligned}$$ Now we will understand firm entry and exit in this industry. There is a larga and unbounded pool of prospects. Exit is free. To enter, firms need to invest fixed cost $f_e \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$. Then, firms draw their initial productivty $\varphi$ from distribution $g(\varphi)$ with support over $(0, \infty)$ and CDF G($\varphi)$. After drawing a bad  $\varphi$, firms may choose to exit freely, or face probability $\delta$ of a bad shock that would force them to exit. The model assumes that ex-ante survival probability and $\mu(\varphi)$ are determined exogenously by $\delta$ and $g(\varphi)$. The modul focuses on constant productivity $\varphi$ and aggregate variables in stationary equilibirum. Thus, $\pi(\varphi)$ is constant. $$v(\varphi)=\max\left\{0,\sum_{t=0}^{\infty}(1-\delta)^t\pi(\varphi)\right\}=\max\left\{0,\frac{\pi(\varphi)}{\delta}\right\}$$ is the value function of a firm, which represents the present value of future flows assuming no time discounting. Thus $\varphi^{*}=\inf\left\{\varphi \mid v(\varphi)>0\right\}$ identifies the cutoff level of firms. This is the lowest productivity allowed for a firm to survive. We call $\pi(varphi^{*})=0$ the zero-profit condition (ZPC). $\pi(0)=-f$ is negative and all firms that draw $\varphi < \varphi^{*}$ exit. For remaining firms $\mu(\varphi)$ is $g(\varphi)$ on $[ \varphi^{*}, \infty)$ $$\mu(\varphi)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{g(\varphi)}{1-G(\varphi^{*})} & \text{if } \varphi \geqslant \varphi^{*} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}$$ The probability of entering the market is $p_{in} \equiv 1-G(\varphi^{*})$. Aggregate productivity $\tilde{\varphi}$ is then $$\tilde{\varphi}(\varphi^{*})=\left[\frac{1}{p_{in}}\int_{\varphi^{*}}^{\infty}\varphi^{\sigma-1}g(\varphi)\,d\varphi\right]^{\frac{1}{\sigma-1}}$$ Therefore average profit $\tilde{\pi}$ and average revenue $\tilde{r}$, much like $\tilde{\varphi}$ are determined by cutoff level $\varphi^{*}$ so $$\begin{aligned}\tilde{r} = \underbrace{r(\tilde{\varphi}) = \left(\frac{\tilde{\varphi}(\varphi^{*})}{\varphi^{*}}\right)^{\sigma-1} r(\varphi^{*})}_{\text{average revenue}} && \tilde{\pi} = \underbrace{\pi(\tilde{\varphi}) = \left(\frac{\tilde{\varphi}(\varphi^{*})}{\varphi^{*}}\right)^{\sigma-1} \frac{r(\varphi^{*})}{\sigma} - f}_{\text{average profit level}}\end{aligned}$$ ZCP implies relationship between $\bar{\pi}$ and $\varphi^{*}$ $$\pi(\varphi^{*}) = 0 \Longleftrightarrow r(\varphi^{*}) = \sigma f \Longleftrightarrow \bar{\pi} = f\left(\left(\frac{\tilde{\varphi}(\varphi^{*})}{\varphi^{*}}\right)^{\sigma-1} - 1\right) = f k(\varphi)$$ Beside cutoff firm, all firms have $\pi \in \mathbb{R}^{+} \Rightarrow \bar{\pi} \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$. Let $$\bar{v} = \sum_{t=0}^{\infty}(1-\delta)^t \bar{\pi} = \left(\frac{1}{\delta}\right)\bar{\pi} = \int_{\varphi^{*}}^{\infty} v(\varphi)\,\mu(\varphi)\,d\varphi$$ represent the present value of average profit flows and average value of firms conditional on entry. Firms will continue to enter as long as the expected value of profits minus entry cost is non-negative. We call this the net value of entry $v_e$ $$v_e = p_{in}\,\bar{v} - f_e = \frac{1 - G(\varphi^{*})}{\delta}\,\bar{\pi} - f_e \;\Rightarrow\; \bar{\pi} = \frac{\delta f_e}{1 - G(\varphi^{*})}$$ We then have the free entry (FE) and ZCP conditions. They represent two different relations that link average profit $\bar{\pi}$ and cutoff productivity $\varphi^{*}$. Notice the FE curve is increasing is cut from above by ZCP at a unique point in $(\varphi, \pi)$ space. This is an equilibrium. $$\begin{aligned}\underbrace{\bar{\pi}=f\,k(\varphi^{*})}_{\text{ZCP}} && \text{and} && \underbrace{\bar{\pi}=\frac{\delta f_e}{1-G(\varphi^{*})}}_{\text{FE}}\end{aligned}$$ Since it is stationary equilibrium, we need a mass $M_e$ of new entrants such that $p_{in} M_e$, the proportion of new entrants who are succesful replace $\delta M$, the mass who exit due to a bad shock i.e. $p_{in} M_e = \delta M$. Then aggregate labor is divided as follows $$L=\underbrace{L_p}_{\text{production}}+\underbrace{L_e}_{\text{entrants}}$$ where $L_p$ is labor used in production and $L_e$ is labor used in investment by new entrants. Payment to productive labor must be $L_p = R-\Pi$, as it is the difference between aggregate revenue and profit. Then, the labor usedfor entrants is $L_e=M_e f_e \Rightarrow$ $$\begin{split}p_{in}M_e=\delta M \Rightarrow M_e=\frac{\delta M}{p_{in}} \\ L_e=\frac{\delta f_e M}{p_{in}}=\left(\frac{\delta f_e}{p_{in}}\right)M=\bar{\pi}M=\Pi \\ L=L_p+L_e=\Pi+L_p=\Pi+R-\Pi=R\end{split}$$ Then we can also determine the mass $M$ of producing firms as follows $$\begin{split}R=M\bar{r} \Rightarrow M=\frac{R}{\bar{r}}=\frac{L}{\sigma(\bar{\pi}+f)} \\ P=M^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}}p(\tilde{\varphi})=\frac{M^{\frac{1}{1-\sigma}}}{\rho\tilde{\varphi}}\end{split}$$ All firm-level variables $\varphi^{*}, \tilde{\varphi}, \bar{\pi},$ and $\bar{r}$ are independent of $L$. Mass of firms $M$ increases proportionally with country size $L$. However $\mu(\varphi)$ and $\varphi^{*}$ are unchanged. $$W = \frac{w}{P} = \frac{1}{P} = P^{-1} = M^{\frac{1}{\sigma-1}} \rho \tilde{\varphi}$$ is welfare per worker. It is higher in bigger countries because of increased variety. Welfare is defined as the purchasing power of the worker. So country size $L$ affects the number of firms but not the selection. We now turn to analyzing trade. Theworld is comprised of $n+1 \geqslant 2$ countries like the one we just described. Firms divide sales between domestic and foreign countries. With no costs, the open economy replicates outcomes of a larger closed economy. There are two trade costs. There are fixed costs to enterimg foreign markets (standards, distribution channels, etc). Let $\tau > 1$ be the amount of units of a good that have to be shipped for 1 unit to arrive at the export destination (iceberg tarde costs). Suppose we study an economy that can trade with $n \geqslant 1$ other countries. For each export destination there is an additional pre-period cost $f_{ex} \in \mathbb{R}^{+}$ to their fixed cost $f$. $$\begin{aligned}p_d(\varphi)=\underbrace{\frac{w}{p\rho}=\frac{1}{\rho\varphi}}_{\text{domestic}} && \text{and} && p_x(\varphi)=\underbrace{\frac{\tau}{\rho\varphi}=\tau p_d(\varphi)}_{\text{foreign}}\end{aligned}$$ are the two pricing rules for their respective markets, while the revenue is $$\begin{aligned}r_d(\varphi)=\underbrace{R(P\rho\varphi)^{\sigma-1}}_{\text{domestic}} && \text{and} && r_x(\varphi)=\underbrace{\tau^{1-\sigma}r_d(\varphi)}_{\text{foreign}}\end{aligned}$$ the, a firm's combined revenue depends on export status $$r(\varphi)=\begin{cases}r_d(\varphi), & \text{if no exports} \\ r_d(\varphi)+n\,r_x(\varphi)=\left(1+n\tau^{1-\sigma}\right)r_d(\varphi) & \text{if it exports}\end{cases}$$ Remember firms earn variable profit $\frac{r_{x}(\varphi)}{\sigma}$ in every period. Firms either export to all countries or they do not export.
